front: What is an interrupt in a microcontroller?
back: An interrupt is a signal that temporarily halts the main program execution and transfers control to an Interrupt Service Routine (ISR) to handle a specific event or condition.

front: What is the difference between polling and interrupt-driven I/O?
back: Polling continuously checks peripheral status (wastes CPU cycles), while interrupt-driven I/O alerts CPU only when needed (efficient, real-time response).

front: What is an Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)?
back: An ISR (or Interrupt Handler) is a special function executed in response to an interrupt, containing code to handle the interrupting event quickly before returning to main program.

front: What is an interrupt vector table?
back: A vector table is a memory array containing addresses of ISRs. Each interrupt source has a dedicated entry pointing to its corresponding handler routine.

front: What are the two main types of interrupts in 8051?
back: The two types are: 1) Hardware/External interrupts (INT0, INT1) triggered by pins, and 2) Timer interrupts (TF0, TF1) triggered by timer overflow.

front: How do you enable interrupts in 8051?
back: Set the EA (Enable All) bit in IE register (IE.7) to globally enable interrupts, then set individual enable bits for specific interrupt sources.

front: What is the IE register in 8051?
back: IE (Interrupt Enable) is an 8-bit register (address A8H) that controls which interrupts are enabled. Format: EA - - ES ET1 EX1 ET0 EX0.
Interrupt latency is the time delay between interrupt request and start of ISR execution. It depends on current instruction completion and priority handling.

front: What is interrupt priority in microcontrollers?
back: Interrupt priority determines the order in which simultaneous interrupts are serviced. Higher priority interrupts can preempt lower priority ones.

front: What is the IP register in 8051?
back: IP (Interrupt Priority) is an 8-bit register (address B8H) that sets priority levels. Format: - - - PS PT1 PX1 PT0 PX0.
A nested interrupt occurs when a higher priority interrupt preempts a currently executing lower priority ISR, allowing multiple interrupt levels.

front: What is the difference between level-triggered and edge-triggered interrupts?
back: Level-triggered: interrupt active as long as pin is high. Edge-triggered: interrupt triggered only on high-to-low or low-to-high transition.

front: What are the external interrupts in 8051?
back: 8051 has two external interrupts: INT0 (P3.2) and INT1 (P3.3). They can be configured as level-triggered or edge-triggered via TCON register.

front: What is the TCON register used for?
back: TCON (Timer Control) manages timer overflow flags and external interrupt types. Bits: TF1, TR1, TF0, TR0, IE1, IT1, IE0, IT0.

front: What happens when an interrupt occurs in 8051?
back: CPU completes current instruction, saves PC on stack, loads ISR address from vector table, executes ISR, then restores PC from stack to resume main program.
